titleStmt.title: Hierarchical Relational Learning for Few-Shot Knowledge Graph Completion

analytic.title: Hierarchical Relational Learning for Few-Shot Knowledge Graph Completion

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Knowledge graphs (KGs) are known for their large scale and knowledge inference ability, but are also notorious for the incompleteness associated with them. Due to the long-tail distribution of the relations in KGs, few-shot KG completion has been proposed as a solution to alleviate incompleteness and expand the coverage of KGs. It aims to make predictions for triplets involving novel relations when only a few training triplets are provided as reference. Previous methods have mostly focused on designing local neighbor aggregators to learn entity-level information and/or imposing sequential dependency assumption at the triplet level to learn meta relation information. However, valuable pairwise tripletlevel interactions and context-level relational information have been largely overlooked for learning meta representations of fewshot relations. In this paper, we propose a hierarchical relational learning method (HiRe) for few-shot KG completion. By jointly capturing three levels of relational information (entity-level, tripletlevel and context-level), HiRe can effectively learn and refine the meta representation of few-shot relations, and consequently generalize very well to new unseen relations. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets validate the superiority of HiRe against other state-of-the-art methods.

div.head: CCS CONCEPTS
div.p: • Computing methodologies → Reasoning about belief and knowledge.

div.head: INTRODUCTION
div.p: Knowledge graphs (KGs) represent a large amount of entities and their relationships via a collection of factual triplets, (ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡), where each triplet expresses the relationship 𝑟 between a head entity ℎ and a tail entity 𝑡. Large-scale KGs [1,19,26,32] can provide powerful knowledge inference capabilities for many intelligent applications, including question answering [38], web search [8] and recommendation systems [33]. As KGs are often built semiautomatically from unstructured data, it has become a well-known fact that most existing KGs are far from complete [6]. This has expedited research on knowledge graph completion, which aims to add new factual triplets to a knowledge graph by predicting missing relations. Following the seminal work of TransE [2] and its extraordinary performance on WN18 [2] and FB15k [2], KG embedding methods and their variants have dominated the task of KG completion. Despite their promising results, conventional KG completion methods are only suitable for predicting relations with large amounts of available triplets. However, real-world KGs suffer from the notorious long-tail problem -a considerable proportion of relations are associated with only very few triplets. For example, about 10% of relations in Wikidata have no more than 10 triplets [36]. As a result, the performance of current KG completion methods significantly drops when predicting for relations with a limited number (few-shot) of training triplets. This issue has prompted a series of research works on few-shot KG completion that focuses on predicting the missing tail entity 𝑡 for query triplets by learning from only K reference triplets about a target relation 𝑟 .

Given a target relation 𝑟 and K reference triplets, the goal of Kshot KG completion is to correctly predict the tail entity 𝑡 for each query triplet (ℎ, 𝑟, ?) using the generalizable knowledge learned from the given reference triplets. Thus, the crucial aspect of fewshot KG completion is to learn meta representation of each few-shot relation from the limited amount of reference triplets that can generalize to novel relations. In essence, we identity three levels of relational information that contribute to learning meta representation of the target relation (see Figure 1). Firstly, each reference triplet is closely related to its wider contexts, providing abundant relevant information. Such context-level information is crucial for enriching entity and relation embeddings. Secondly, as the quantity of reference triplets is limited for each few-shot relation, it is crucial to learn meta relation information that can inherently capture triplet-level commonality among reference triplets. Thirdly, the learned meta representation of the given relation should well generalize to unseen triplets (i.e. query triplets), which calls for entity-level relational learning.
div.formula: ℎ 𝑡𝑡 ℎ 𝑡𝑡 … h 2 𝑡𝑡 2 h 𝑘𝑘 𝑡𝑡 𝑘𝑘 h 1 t 1 h 2 t 2(
div.p: To exploit relational information for few-shot KG completion, a series of research works have been proposed. The pioneering work GMatching [36] designs a neighbor encoder to leverage local neighborhood structure for learning better representation of each entity in the given reference triplet. Subsequent works (e.g., MetaR [3], FSRL [39], GANA [21], and FAAN [24]) extend one-shot setting of GMatching to more shots, with key challenges to extract relation-specific meta information from K reference triplets. MetaR simply average the embe3ddings of all the K reference triplets, while FSRL [39], GANA [21], and FAAN [24]) utilize recurrent processors composed of LSTMs [14] to learn meta relation representations. Although these methods are found to achieve empirical improvements, they fundamentally neglect the fact that reference triplets are order-invariant, thus violating the property of permutation-invariance. As a result, the unrealistic assumption of sequential dependency jeopardizes the model's ability to capture complex interactions among the reference triplets. Table 1 lists the relational information utilized by existing KG completion methods. In summary, existing works have primarily focused on designing local neighbor aggregators to learn entity-level information and/or imposing sequential assumption at the triplet level to learn meta relation information. However, the potential of leveraging pairwise triplet-level interactions and context-level relational information has been largely unexplored.

In this paper, we propose a Hierarchical Relational learning framework (HiRe) for few-shot KG completion. HiRe jointly models three levels of relations (entity-level, triplet-level, and contextlevel relations) within each few-shot task as mutually reinforcing sources of information to generalize to few-shot relations. Firstly, we propose a contrastive learning based context-level relational learning method to learn expressive entity/relation embeddings by modeling correlations between the target triplet and its true/false contexts. We argue that the triplet itself has a close relation with its wider contexts, especially the semantic structures among neighboring triplets anchored at the target triplet. Taking Figure 3 as an example, jointly considering the wider context shared

by head/tail entity would reveal crucial information -both "Lionel Messi" and "Sergio Agüero" playFor "Argentina's National Team"for determining whether the relation workWith holds between the given entity pair ("Lionel Messi", "Sergio Agüero"). Motivated by this, HiRe employs a contrastive loss to learn better entity embeddings, based on the idea that a given triplet should be pulled close to its true context, but pushed apart from its false contexts in the embedding space. In doing so, we could imbue entity embeddings with the contextual knowledge learned from the wider contexts of the target triplet. Secondly, we propose a transformer-based meta relation learner (MRL) to perform triplet-level relation learning for the meta representation of the given relation 𝑟 . Taking advantage of the set transformer [16], our proposed MRL is capable of capturing pairwise interactions among reference triplets, while preserving the permutation-invariance property and being insensitive to the size of the reference set. Thirdly, we propose a meta representation based embedding learner named MTransD to refine the learned meta relation representation at the entity level following the paradigm of KG translational models [2,15,34]. By forcing the learned meta representation of the target relation holds between unseen query triplets, entity-level relations can be captured and generalize to novel relations. Lastly, we adopt a MAML-based training strategy [9] to optimize HiRe on each meta task within a unified framework. By performing relational learning at three granular levels, our proposed HiRe framework offers significant advantages for extracting expressive meta relation representations and improving model generalizability for few-shot KG completion. We summarize our contributions as follows:

• We identify three levels of relational information that are essential for learning meta representation of a given relation for few-shot KG completion. • We propose a hierarchical relational learning framework (HiRe) to learn a more stereoscopic and generalizable representation space for few-shot KG completion.
• Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets validate the efficacy of HiRe, demonstrating its superiority over stateof-the-art methods.

div.head: RELATED WORK
div.p: In this section, we review related works from three main streams:
(1) relational learning in knowledge graphs; (2) few-shot learning on knowledge graphs; and (3) contrastive learning on graphs.

div.head: Relational Learning in Knowledge Graphs
div.p: Recent advances in graph deep learning has accelerated the development of embedding-based methods for KG completion. This line of research focuses on utilizing relational information available in KGs to learn a unified low-dimensional embedding space for the input triplets, where each entity is projected as a 𝑘-dimensional vector and the relation 𝑟 between the entity pair (ℎ, 𝑡) serves as the translation from ℎ to 𝑡. A scoring function is then used to measure the quality of the translation and to learn a unified embedding space for all triplets.

The pioneering work TransE [2] is the first to propose the aforementioned idea of using relation 𝑟 as translation in the embedding space, i.e. h + r ≈ t when (ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡) ∈ T P. Inspired by TransE, TransH [34] moves forward to project entities into different relationspecific hyperplanes, enabling the learning of distinct entity representations when connected by different relations. TransR [17] builds distinct embedding spaces for entities and relations in order to allow different relations to focus on different aspects of entities. ComplEx [30] uses a bilinear scoring function to model asymmetric relations in a complex vector space. RotatE [28] further improves the modeling of relation patterns by defining each relation as a rotation in the complex space. ConvE [4] and ConvKB [20] take advantage of convolution operators to enhance the learning entity and relation embeddings. However, the aforementioned KG embedding methods require a large number of triplets for each relation to find the unified embedding space during training. The performance of these methods can significantly degrade at few-shot settings, where only very few triplets are available for each relation.

div.head: Few-shot KG Completion
div.p: Existing few-shot KG completion works can be grouped into two main categories: (1) Metric learning based methods: GMatching [36] is the first work to formulate few-shot (one-shot) KG completion. GMatching is comprised of two main parts: a neighbor encoder and a matching processor. The neighbor encoder aggregates the one-hop neighbors of any given entity, and the matching processor compares similarity between query and reference entity pairs. FSRL [39] relaxes the setting to more shots and explores how to integrate the information learned from multiple reference triplets. FAAN [24] is the first to propose a dynamic attention mechanism for one-hop neighbors adapting to the different relations which connect them. (2) Meta learning based methods: MetaR [3] is a meta learning approach that transfers relation-specific meta information to enhance the model learning capacity. However, it generates meta relation representations by simply averaging the representations of all reference triplets. GANA [21] puts more emphasis on neighboring information and accordingly proposes a gated and attentive neighbor aggregator. FSRL [39], GANA [21], and FAAN [24]) utilize recurrent processors composed of LSTMs [14] to learn meta relation representations, but they impose an unrealistic sequential dependency assumption when modeling reference triplets.

Although these methods have achieved state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets, they fail to capture valuable pairwise triplet-level interactions and context-level relational information. Our work is proposed to fill this important research gap in the literature. candidate set for the potential tail entity of ( h𝑗 , 𝑟, ?)
div.formula: N 𝑒 neighborhood of entity 𝑒 C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 )
div.p: context of triplet (ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡)

div.head: Contrastive Learning on Graphs
div.p: Contrastive learning is a self-supervised learning scheme that measures similarity and difference between instances. The general goal of contrastive learning is to contrast positive pairs against negative pairs, i.e. pulling similar (positive) instances closer and pushing different (negative) instances apart [7,10]. Successful contrastive methods follow the instance discrimination principle that pairs instances based on whether they are derived from the same instance (i.e. positive pairs) or not (i.e. negative pairs).

Inspired by the success in computer vision and natural language processing [5,13,18,22,25,29,35], contrastive methods have been recently proposed to learn expressive node embeddings on graphs. In general, these methods train a graph encoder that produces node embeddings and a discriminator which distinguishes node embedding pairs that are similar from those that are dissimilar. For instance, DGI [31] trains a node encoder to maximize mutual information between patch representations and corresponding highlevel summaries of graphs. InfoGraph [27] demonstrates that it is essential to maximize the mutual information between the representation of the entire graph and the representations of substructures. The work of [12] proposes to use contrast encodings from one-hop neighbors and a graph diffusion, achieving state-of-the-art performance on both node and graph classification tasks. GCC [23] adopts InfoNCE loss [22] and presents a graph-based contrastive learning framework for downstream tasks.

In KGs, we note that positive and negative pairs naturally exist in the problem of few-shot KG completion. However, the potential of contrastive learning in this task is under-explored. In our work, we adopt the idea of contrastive learning at the context level to capture correlations between a target triplet and its wider context. This enables to enrich the expressiveness of entity embeddings in KGs and improving model generalisation for few-shot KG completion. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to integrate contrastive learning with KG embedding learning for few-shot KG completion.

div.head: PROBLEM FORMULATION
div.p: In this section, we formally define the few-shot KG completion task and corresponding problem setting. First we give a summary of notations used in this paper in Table 2. Definition 1. Knowledge Graph G. A knowledge graph can be denoted as G = {E, R, T P}. E and R are the entity set and the relation set, respectively. T P = {(ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡) ∈ E × R × E} denotes the set of all triplets in the knowledge graph.
div.formula: Definition 2. Few-shot KG Completion. Given (𝑖) a knowl- edge graph G = {E, R, T P}, (𝑖𝑖) a reference set S 𝑟 = {(ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑡 𝑖 ) ∈ E × E |(ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑟, 𝑡 𝑖 ) ∈ T P} corresponding to a given relation 𝑟 ∈ R,
div.p: where |S 𝑟 | = K, (𝑖𝑖𝑖) a query set Q 𝑟 = {( h𝑗 , 𝑟, ?)} that also corresponds to the given relation 𝑟 , the goal of K-shot KG completion task is to predict the true tail entities for triplets from Q 𝑟 based on the knowledge learned from G and S 𝑟 . To be specific, for each triplet ( h𝑗 , 𝑟, ?) ∈ Q 𝑟 , a set of candidates C h𝑗 ,𝑟 for the potential tail entity are provided and the goal is to rank the true tail entity highest among C h𝑗 ,𝑟 .

As the definition states, few-shot KG completion is a relationspecific task. The ultimate goal of a few-shot KG completion model is to correctly predict new triplets about a relation 𝑟 when only a few triplets about 𝑟 are available. Therefore, the training process is based on the unit of tasks, where each task is to predict new triplets for a given relation 𝑟 , denoted as T 𝑟 . Each meta training task T 𝑟 corresponds to a given relation 𝑟 and is composed of a reference set S 𝑟 and a query set Q 𝑟 , i.e. T 𝑟 = {S 𝑟 , Q 𝑟 }:

where M is the size of Q 𝑟 . Mathematically, the training set can be denoted as T 𝑡𝑟𝑎𝑖𝑛 = {T 𝑖 } 𝐼 𝑖=1 . The test set T 𝑡𝑒𝑠𝑡 = {T 𝑗 } 𝐽 𝑗=1 can be similarly denoted. Note that all triplets corresponding to the relations in test set are unseen during training, i.e. T 𝑡𝑟𝑎𝑖𝑛 ∩T 𝑡𝑒𝑠𝑡 = ∅.

div.head: THE PROPOSED METHOD
div.p: In this section, we introduce the overall structure of our proposed learning framework in details. We target at the K-shot KG completion task, where each meta task consists of K reference triplets for one given relation 𝑟 as the reference set.

Based on our observation that three levels of relational information inherently exist in KGs, we break down the few-shot KG completion task into three sub-tasks: (1) Augmenting the entity representation via aggregating neighboring information, (2) Learning meta representation of a given relation 𝑟 via modeling interactions among the reference set, and (3) Measuring the similarity between query and reference set via matching functions. As discussed earlier, previous works have mostly focused on designing local neighbor aggregators to learn entity-level relational information and building sequential dependencies to capture triplet-level relationships, while crucial pairwise interactions among reference triplets and context-level relational information have been largely overlooked.

To fill this research gap, we propose to perform three levels of relational learning within each meta task (context-level, tripletlevel and entity-level), to learn a more stereoscopic and generalizable representation space for few-shot KG completion. Fig. 2 shows an overview of our proposed hierarchical relational learning (HiRe) framework. HiRe is composed of three major components:

div.head: Contrastive learning based context-level relational learning
div.p: Given a reference set S 𝑟 with only K training triplets, existing works (e.g., [21,36]) seek to learn better head/tail entity embedding by aggregating information from its respective local neighbors, and then concatenate the embeddings of head entity and tail entity to represent each triplet. Although treating the neighborhoods of head/tail entity separately is a common practice in homogeneous graphs, we argue that it is not suitable for few-shot KG completion. Consider the following example in the context of interactions among professional football players, as illustrated in Fig. 3. Here, we would like to infer the potential relation between an entity pair ("Lionel Messi", "Sergio Agüero"). Previous works typically treat the neighborhoods of head entity "Lionel Messi" and tail entity "Sergio Agüero" separately, as shown in Figure 3a. Each neighboring pair, such as ("Paris", liveIn), partially contributes to the embedding of the anchor entity "Lionel Messi". However, if we jointly look into the wider contexts of both head entity and tail entity (see Figure 3b), the crucial fact that both "Lionel Messi" and "Sergio Agüero" playFor "Argentina's National Team" would reveal critical information when deciding if the relation workWith holds between the given entity pair. Clearly, treating neighborhoods of head/tail entity separately would lead to loss of critical information. This motivates us to jointly consider neighborhoods of head/tail entity. The idea of joining neighborhoods also inspires us to leverage relational knowledge in the context of a given triplet. Intuitively, a given triplet should be close to its wider context and be apart from the context of other triplets or its corrupted contexts. Based on the above insights, we propose a contrastive learning based context-level relational learning method for learning expressive entity embeddings. The idea is to enrich entity embeddings by capturing correlations between a target triplet and its wider context. Towards this goal, for each triplet (ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡) from the reference set, we construct its true context as the union of the neighborhoods of head entity ℎ and tail entity 𝑡, and synthesize a group of false contexts by randomly corrupting the entity/relation within the true context. For the target triplet, we propose a context encoder to learn the embeddings of its true/false contexts based on self-attention mechanism so that important neighbors within the context would be given a larger weight. Furthermore, a contrastive loss is employed to pull close the triplet towards its true context and push apart from its false contexts. As such, we can imbue the embedding of the target triplet with its context-level relational information, thus enriching the expressiveness of its head and tail entity embeddings.

Mathematically, given a target triplet (ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡), its context is denoted as C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 ) = N ℎ ∪ N 𝑡 , where N ℎ = {(𝑟 𝑖 , 𝑡 𝑖 )|(ℎ, 𝑟 𝑖 , 𝑡 𝑖 ) ∈ T P} and N 𝑡 = {(𝑟 𝑗 , 𝑡 𝑗 )|(𝑡, 𝑟 𝑗 , 𝑡 𝑗 ) ∈ T P}. The goal of context-level relational learning is to capture correlations between the given triplet (ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡) and its context C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 ) . To capture context-level relational information, we propose a multi-head self-attention (MSA) based context encoder, which models the interactions among the given context C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 ) and assigns larger weights to more important Specifically, given a target triplet (ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡) and its context C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 ) , each relation-entity tuple (𝑟 𝑖 , 𝑡 𝑖 ) ∈ C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 ) is first encoded as:
div.formula: re 𝑖 = r 𝑖 ⊕ t 𝑖 ,(3)
div.p: where r 𝑖 ∈ R 𝑑 and t 𝑖 ∈ R 𝑑 is the relation and entity embedding, respectively. An MSA block is then employed to uncover the underlying relationship within the context and generate context embedding c: c 0 = [re 1 ; re 2 ; ..., ; re 𝐾 ], 𝐾 = |C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 ) | (4)
div.formula: 𝛼 = softmax(MSA(c 0 )),(5) c
div.p: where c 0 is the concatenation of the embeddings of all relationentity tuples, |𝑥 | is the size of set 𝑥, and softmax stands for the softmax function. In doing so, the self-attention scores among all relation-entity tuples from C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 ) can be computed by Eq. 5. Those tuples with higher correlations and similarities would be given larger weights and contribute more towards the embedding of C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 ) .

Based on the idea that the triplet should be close to its true context but far away from its false contexts, we further synthesize a group of false contexts { C(ℎ,𝑟,𝑡)𝑖 } by corrupting the true relation-entity pairs in C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 ) . Specifically, we randomly corrupt the relation or entity of each relation-entity tuple (𝑟 𝑖 , 𝑡 𝑖 ) ∈ C (ℎ,𝑟,𝑡 ) to construct false contexts. Similarly, each false context C(ℎ,𝑟,𝑡)𝑖 can be learned by the context encoder as c𝑖 . Then, we use a contrastive loss to pull close the embedding of the target triplet with its true context and to push away from its false contexts. The contrastive loss function can be defined as follows:
div.formula: L 𝑐 = -log exp(sim(h ⊕ t, c)/𝜏) 𝑁 𝑖=0 exp(sim(h ⊕ t, c𝑖 )/𝜏) , (7
div.p: where 𝑁 is the number of false contexts for triplet (ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡), 𝜏 denotes the temperature parameter, h ⊕ t indicates the triplet embedding represented as the concatenation of its head and tail entity embeddings, sim(𝑥, 𝑦) measures the cosine similarity between 𝑥 and 𝑦. As a result, we can inject context-level knowledge into entity embeddings with more emphasise on key elements within the context of the given triplet.

div.head: Transformer-based triplet-level relational learning
div.p: After obtaining the embeddings of all reference triplet, the next focus is to learn meta relation representation for the target relation 𝑟 . For this purpose, the state-of-the-art models (e.g. FSRL [39], GANA [21]) utilize recurrent processors comprised of LSTMs, based on a sequential dependency assumption on reference triplets. Despite their excellent empirical results, these models violate two important properties here. First, the model should be insensitive to the size of reference set (i.e. few-shot size K). Second, the triplets in the reference set should be permutation-invariant since they do not have sequential dependencies.

To address these issues, our goal is to better model triplet-level relational information, so that complex interactions among reference triplets can be captured to learn generic meta relational knowledge.

To this end, we propose a transformer-based meta relation learner (MRL) that effectively models pairwise interactions among reference triplets and learn generic meta representation of the given relation 𝑟 that generalizes well to new unseen relations. Inspired by set transformer [16], we design our MRL based on the idea of set attention block (SAB) to leverage triplet-triplet interactions while conforming to the two aforementioned properties. Generally, an SAB is designed to take a set of objects as input and perform selfattention mechanism between the elements. Our proposed MRL takes advantage of the SAB to model pairwise triplet-triplet interactions within S 𝑟 so as to cultivate the ability to learn generalizable meta representation of the target relation.

Mathematically, given a meta training task T 𝑟 targeting at relation 𝑟 , our proposed MRL takes the head/tail entity pairs from its reference set as input, i.e. {(ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑡 𝑖 ) ∈ S 𝑟 }. Each reference triplet is encoded as:
div.formula: x 𝑖 = h 𝑖 ⊕ t 𝑖 ,(8)
div.p: where h 𝑖 ∈ R 𝑑 and t 𝑖 ∈ R 𝑑 are embeddings of entity ℎ 𝑖 and tail entity 𝑡 𝑖 with dimension 𝑑. x ⊕ y denotes the concatenation of two vectors x and y. Since all reference triplets are associated with the same relation 𝑟 , our proposed MRL aims to capture the commonality among these reference triplets and obtain meta representation for relation 𝑟 . To comprehensively incorporate triplet-level relational information in the reference set, we leverage an SAB on the embeddings of all reference triplets from S 𝑟 :

where x 𝑖 denotes the 𝑖-th reference triplet. The output of SAB has the same size of the input X, but contains pairwise triplet-triplet interactions among X. The transformed embeddings of reference triplets, X', are then fed into a two-layer MLP to obtain the meta representation R T 𝑟 , i.e.:
div.formula: R T 𝑟 = 1 K K ∑︁ 𝑖=1 𝑀𝐿𝑃 (X'),(11)
div.p: where the meta representation R T 𝑟 is generated by averaging the transformed embeddings of all reference triplets. This ensures that R T 𝑟 contains the fused pairwise triplet-triplet interactions among the reference set in a permutation-invariant manner.

div.head: Meta representation based entity-level relational learning
div.p: So far we have performed context-level and triplet-level relational learning to obtain the meta representation R T 𝑟 for the target relation 𝑟 based on the reference set. A crucial aspect of few-shot KG completion is to warrant the generalizability of the learned meta representation. Intuitively, the learned R T 𝑟 should hold between (h 𝑖 , t 𝑖 ) if ℎ 𝑖 and 𝑡 𝑖 are associated with 𝑟 . This motivates us to refine R T 𝑟 under the constraints of true/false entity pairs. To this end, we design a score function based on the meta representation R T 𝑟 following the paradigm of KG translational models [2,17,34] to satisfy such constraints. As pointed out by TransD [15], the diversity of entities and relations should be considered simultaneously and a mapping matrix for each entity-relation pair should be constructed in a dynamic manner. Therefore, this meta representation based embedding constraint is referred to as MTransD for effectively building the meta translational relationship at entity-entity level. Given a target relation 𝑟 and its corresponding reference set S 𝑟 , after obtaining its meta representation R T 𝑟 , we can now calculate a score for each entity pair (ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑡 𝑖 ) ∈ S 𝑟 by projecting the embedding of head/tail entity into a latent space determined by its corresponding entities and relation simultaneously. Mathematically, the projection process and the score function can be formulated as:
div.formula: h 𝑝𝑖 = r 𝑝𝑖 h ⊺ 𝑝𝑖 h 𝑖 + I 𝑚×𝑛 h 𝑖 ,(12)
t 𝑝𝑖 = r 𝑝𝑖 t ⊺ 𝑝𝑖 t 𝑖 + I 𝑚×𝑛 t 𝑖 , (13) score(ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑡 𝑖 ) =∥ h 𝑝𝑖 + R T 𝑟 -t 𝑝𝑖 ∥ 2 ,(14)
div.p: where ∥ x ∥ 2 represents the L2 norm of vector x. h 𝑖 and t 𝑖 are the head and tail entity embeddings, h 𝑝𝑖 and t 𝑝𝑖 are their corresponding projection vectors. r 𝑝𝑖 is the projection vector of R T 𝑟 and I 𝑚×𝑛 is an identity matrix. This way, the projection matrices of each head/tail entity are determined by both the entity itself and the relation that connects it. As a result, the projected tail entity embedding should be closest to the projected head entity embedding after being translated by R T 𝑟 . That is to say, for these triplets associated with relation 𝑟 , R T 𝑟 should hold between h 𝑝𝑖 and t 𝑝𝑖 in the projection space. Considering the whole reference set, we can further define a loss function as follows:
div.formula: L (S 𝑟 ) = ∑︁ (ℎ 𝑖 ,𝑡 𝑖 ) ∈S 𝑟 𝑚𝑎𝑥 {0, score(ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑡 𝑖 ) + 𝛾 -score(ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑡 ′ 𝑖 )}, (15
div.p: where 𝛾 is a hyper-parameter that determines the margin to separate positive pairs from negative pairs. score(ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑡 ′ 𝑖 ) calculates the score of a negative pair (ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑡 ′ 𝑖 ) which results from negative sampling of the positive pair (ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑡 𝑖 ) ∈ S 𝑟 , i.e.(ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑟, 𝑡 ′ 𝑖 ) ∉ G. Till now, we have obtained meta relation representation R T 𝑟 for each few-shot relation 𝑟 , along with a loss function on the reference set.

div.head: MAML-based training strategy
div.p: Noting that L (S 𝑟 ) from Eq. 15 is task-specific and should be minimized on the target task T 𝑟 , we adopt a model agnostic meta learning (MAML) based training strategy [9] to optimize the parameters on each task T 𝑟 . The obtained loss on the reference set L (S 𝑟 ) is not used to train the whole model but to update intermediate parameters. The learned R T 𝑟 can be further refined based on the gradient of L (S 𝑟 ):
div.formula: 𝑅 ′ T 𝑟 = R T 𝑟 -𝑙 𝑟 ∇ R T𝑟 L (S 𝑟 ),(16)
div.p: where 𝑙 𝑟 is the learning rate. Furthermore, for each target relation T 𝑟 , the projection vectors h 𝑝𝑖 , r 𝑝𝑖 and t 𝑝𝑖 can also be optimized in the same manner of MAML so that the model can generalize and adapt to a new target relation. Following MAML, the projection vectors are updated as follows:
div.formula: h ′ 𝑝𝑖 = h 𝑝𝑖 -𝑙 𝑟 ∇ h 𝑝𝑖 L (S 𝑟 ),(17)
r ′ 𝑝𝑖 = r 𝑝𝑖 -𝑙 𝑟 ∇ r 𝑝𝑖 L (S 𝑟 ),(18)
t ′ 𝑝𝑖 = t 𝑝𝑖 -𝑙 𝑟 ∇ t 𝑝𝑖 L (S 𝑟 ),(19)
div.p: With the updated parameters, we can project and score each entity pair (ℎ 𝑗 , 𝑡 𝑗 ) from the query set Q 𝑟 following the same scheme as reference set and obtain the entity-level loss function L (Q 𝑟 ):
div.formula: h 𝑝 𝑗 = r ′ 𝑝 𝑗 h ′ 𝑝 𝑗 ⊺ h 𝑗 + I 𝑚×𝑛 h 𝑗 ,(20)
t 𝑝 𝑗 = r ′ 𝑝 𝑗 t ′ 𝑝 𝑗 ⊺ t 𝑗 + I 𝑚×𝑛 t 𝑗 ,(21) score
(ℎ 𝑗 , 𝑡 𝑗 ) =∥ h 𝑝 𝑗 + 𝑅 ′ T 𝑟 -t 𝑝 𝑗 ∥ 2 ,(22)
L (Q 𝑟 ) = ∑︁ (ℎ 𝑗 ,𝑡 𝑗 ) ∈ Q 𝑟 max{0, score(ℎ 𝑗 , 𝑡 𝑗 ) + 𝛾 -score(ℎ 𝑗 , 𝑡 ′ 𝑗 )},(23)
div.p: where (ℎ 𝑗 , 𝑡 ′ 𝑗 ) is also a negative triplet generated in the same way as (ℎ 𝑖 , 𝑡 ′ 𝑖 ). The optimization objective for training the whole model is to minimize L (Q 𝑟 ) and L 𝑐 together, i.e.:
div.formula: L = L (Q 𝑟 ) + 𝜆L 𝑐 ,(24)
div.p: where 𝜆 is a trade-off hyper-parameter that balances the contributions of L (Q 𝑟 ) and L 𝑐 .

div.head: EXPERIMENTS
div.p: In this section, we validate the performance of our proposed method through comparisons against the state-of-the-arts on KG benchmark datasets, followed by detailed ablation study and parameter analysis.

div.head: Datasets and Evaluation Metrics
div.p: We conduct experiments on two widely-used few-shot KG completion datasets, NELL-One and Wiki-One, for the evaluation of the proposed method against other baselines. We follow the experimental protocol proposed by GMatching [36], where relations associated with more than 50 but less than 500 triplets are chosen for few-shot completion tasks. For each target relation, the candidate entity set also follows the setting provided by GMatching [36]. The statistics of both datasets are provided in Table 3. We use 51/5/11 and 133/16/34 tasks for training/validation/test on NELL-One and Wiki-One, respectively. We report both MRR (mean reciprocal rank) and Hits@n (𝑛 = 1, 5, 10) on both datasets for the evaluation of performance. MRR is the mean reciprocal rank of the correct entities, and Hits@n is the ratio of correct entities that rank in top 𝑛. We compare the proposed method against other baseline methods in 1-shot and 5-shot settings, which are the most common settings in the literature.

div.head: Baselines
div.p: For evaluation, we compare our proposed method against state-ofthe-art baselines, which are grouped into two categories below: Conventional KG completion methods: This category of methods focus on modeling relational structures in KG to learn expressive entity/relation embeddings. We adopt four widely used methods as baselines, including TransE [2], TransH [34], Dist-Mult [37] and ComplEx [30]. We use OpenKE [11] to reproduce the experimental results of these conventional models with the hyper-parameters reported in the original papers.

Few-shot KG completion methods: This category of baselines are designed specifically for few-shot KG completion and have achieved state-of-the-art performance on NELL-One and Wiki-One datasets. For MetaR [3] (both In-Train and Pre-Train) and FAAN [24], we directly report results obtained from the original papers. For GMatching [36], we report the results provided by MetaR [3] for both 1-shot and 5-shot. Because FSRL [39] was initially reported in different experimental settings, where the candidate set is much smaller, we report the results re-implemented by FAAN [24] under the same setting with other methods. Due to the fact that the reproduced results of GANA [21] is less competitive, we leave GANA out in our comparison.

div.head: Implementation Details
div.p: For fair comparison, we use the entity and relation embeddings pretrained by TransE [2] on both datasets, released by GMatching [36], for the initialization of HiRe. Following the literature, the embedding dimension is set to 100 and 50 for NELL-One and Wiki-One, respectively. On both datasets, we set the number of SAB to 1 and each SAB contains 1 self-attention head. We apply drop path to avoid overfitting with a drop rate set to 0.2. The maximum number of neighbors of one given entity is set to 50, the same as prior works. For all experiments except for the sensitivity test on the trade-off parameter 𝜆 from Eq. 24, 𝜆 is set to 0.05. The number of false contexts for each reference triplet is set to 1. The margin 𝛾 from Eq. 15 is set to 1. We apply mini-batch gradient descent to train the network with a batch size of 1, 024 for both datasets. Adam optimizer is used with learning rate of 0.001. We evaluate Table 4: Comparison of our proposed HiRe against other state-of-the-art methods on Nell-One and Wiki-One. MetaR-I and MetaR-P indicates the In-train and Pre-train of MetaR [3], respectively.

Nell-One Wiki-One Methods MRR Hits@10 Hits@5 Hits@1 MRR Hits@10 Hits@5 Hits@1 1-shot 5-shot 1-shot 5-shot 1-shot 5-shot 1-shot 5-shot 1-shot 5-shot 1-shot 5-shot 1-shot 5-shot 1-shot 5 Ablation on ↓ Components 3-shot 5-shot MTransD MRL Context MRR Hits@10 Hits@5 Hits@1 MRR Hits@10 Hits@5 Hits@

div.head: Comparison with state-of-the-art methods
div.p: We first present the comparison of HiRe and baselines on NELL-One and Wiki-One in Table 4. To better illustrate the effectiveness of HiRe under various scenarios, we conduct experiments under 1-shot and 5-shot settings, following the literature. From the overall results, we can conclude with the following observations:

• Our HiRe method outperforms all baseline methods under both 1-shot and 5-shot settings on both datasets, which validates the efficacy of our hierarchical relational learning framework for few-shot KG completion. of effectively leveraging stereoscopic relational information and amplifying the advantage of exploiting multi-level relations within the meta task. • As for performance gains in terms of MRR, Hits@10, Hits@5, and Hits@1, HiRe surpasses the second best performer by 3.8%, 7.1%, 6.7%, and 1.4% in 1-shot setting, and by 2.7%, 8.3%, 7.5%, and 0.7% in 5-shot setting on NELL-One. For performance gains on Wiki-One, HiRe outperforms the second best method by 0.8%, 2.9%, 0.8%, and 0.5% in 1-shot setting, and by 3.0%, 3.3%, 2.4%, and 3.8% in 5-shot setting. As a whole, HiRe achieves large performance improvements in terms of Hits@10, proving that taking hierarchical relational information into consideration enhances the model's generalizability and leads to an overall improvement in performance.

div.head: Ablation Study
div.p: Our proposed HiRe framework is composed of three key components: (

div.head: Impact of Hyper-parameter 𝑁
div.p: HiRe uses 𝑁 false contexts for a given triplet for the contrastive loss. To study the impact of 𝑁 , we conduct a sensitivity test on Wiki-One under 1-shot, 3-shot, and 5-shot settings. We set 𝑁 as 1, 2, 4, and 6. As shown in Fig. 5, HiRe achieves the best result when 𝑁 =1 on all settings, and its performance slightly drops when 𝑁 =2. As the number of false contexts continues to increase, the performance of HiRe drops accordingly. One main reason is that, when only one true context is available for the given triplet, increasing the number of false contexts would accelerate the separation of true and false contexts, leading to the result that the model converges too quickly on the contrastive loss.

div.head: Impact of Hyper-parameter 𝜆
div.p: As discussed in Section 4.4, HiRe employs a hyper-parameter 𝜆 to balance the contribution of the margin loss and the contrastive loss in the overall loss function (Eq. 24). To identify the best value of 𝜆, we conduct a sensitivity test on Wiki-One under 1-shot, 3-shot and 5-shot settings. Due to the fact that the value of contrastive loss is significantly larger than the value of margin loss, the trade-off hyper-parameter should be small to retain the supervision from the margin loss effective. Therefore, we searched the best 𝜆 between 0 and 0.5 with an increment of 0.1. Finally, we set 𝜆 to 0.05 for the best performance. As shown in Fig. 4, the contrastive loss effectively improves the performance in a wide range, demonstrating the efficacy of the proposed contrastive learning based context-level relational learning.

div.head: CONCLUSION
div.p: This paper presents a hierarchical relational learning framework (HiRe) for few-shot KG completion. We investigate the limitations of current few-shot KG completion methods and identify that jointly capturing three levels of relational information is crucial for enriching entity and relation embeddings, which ultimately leads to better meta representation of the target relation and model generalizability. To be specific, HiRe learns context-level relational information by modeling the contrastive correlation between a target triplet and its true/false contexts and captures pairwise tripletlevel relational information via a transformer-based meta relation learner. The learned entity/relation embeddings are further refined via MTransD at the entity level. A MAML-based training strategy is adopted to optimize HiRe on each meta task within a unified framework. Experimental results on two commonly used benchmark datasets show that HiRe outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating its superiority and efficacy for few-shot KG completion. The ablation study and hyper-parameter sensitivity study illustrate the significance of the key components of HiRe.

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: (a) Context-level relational information: the target triplet is closely related to its wider context; (b) Triplet-level relational information: reference triplets share commonalities; (c) Entity-level relational information: meta relation should hold between query triplets.
Contrastive learning based context-level relational learning; (2) Transformer-based triplet-level relational learning; (3) Meta representation based entity-level relational learning.

figure.head: Figure 2 :Figure 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 2: An overview of our proposed HiRe framework. Given a target relation 𝑟 and its corresponding reference set S 𝑟 and query set Q 𝑟 , we employ a contrastive loss L 𝑐 between the true/false contexts and the anchor triplet (take (ℎ 1 , 𝑟, 𝑡 1 ) as an example) via our proposed contrastive learning based context-level relational learning method. The meta representation of the target relation R T 𝑟 is learned by our Transformer-based meta relation learner (MRL), capturing pairwise triplet-level relational information. Lastly, MTransD refines the learned meta relation representation at the entity level constrained by L (S 𝑟 ). The whole learning framework is optimized by a MAML-based training strategy.

figure.head: Figure 4 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 4: The impact of different 𝜆 in Eq. 24. 𝜆 = 0 means that we remove the contrastive learning based context-level relational learning.

figure.head: Figure 5 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 5: Hyper-parameter sensitivity study of the number of false contexts.

figure.head: Table 1 :
figure.figDesc: A summary of few-shot KG completion methods based on relational information used.

row.cell: E, R, T P entity, relation and triplet sets of a knowledge graph
embeddings of ℎ, 𝑟 and 𝑡
few-shot task corresponding to relation 𝑟
reference set corresponding to relation 𝑟
query set corresponding to relation 𝑟

figure.figDesc: 𝒯𝒯 𝑟𝑟 -𝛁𝛁 ℛ 𝒯𝒯𝑟𝑟 ℒ 𝒮𝒮 𝑟𝑟

row.cell: ℒ 𝒮𝒮 𝑟𝑟 𝛁𝛁 ℛ 𝒯𝒯𝑟𝑟 ℒ 𝒮𝒮 𝑟𝑟 MTransD 𝓛𝓛 𝓠𝓠 𝒓𝒓 MTransD' (updated) ℛ 𝒯𝒯 𝑟𝑟 ℛ′ 𝒯𝒯 𝑟𝑟 = Transformer-based MRL

figure.head: Table 3 :
figure.figDesc: Statistics of datasets. Each row contains the number of corresponding relations, entities, triplets and tasks for each dataset.

figure.head: Table 5 :
figure.figDesc: Ablation study of HiRe under 3-shot and 5-shot settings on Wiki-One.
